title: Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
text:
2.1 Data Source
The datasets used in this project are collected from two primary sources:

Transfermarkt: Used to obtain financial and squad-related information such as total squad value, average player value, average age, squad size, and number of foreign players.
Türkiye Futbol Federasyonu: Used to collect official league performance statistics such as points, wins, draws, losses, goals scored, goals conceded, and league rankings.

The data has been collected through web scraping techniques and manually verified for consistency. The python script used can be downloaded here

title: Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
text:
2.2 General Information About Data
The project utilizes two main datasets covering a 20-season period from 2004–2005 to 2024–2025.

Dataset 1: Financial and Squad Data (superlig_kadro_verileri)

This dataset contains team-level financial and structural information for each season:
Takim (Team name) Sezon (Season) Kadro_Sayisi (Squad size) Ortalama_Yas (Average age) Yabanci_Oyuncu_Sayisi (Number of foreign players) Ortalama_Piyasa_Degeri_Euro (Average player market value) Toplam_Piyasa_Degeri_Euro (Total squad market value)

Dataset 2: League Performance Data (superlig_puan_durumu_verileri)

This dataset contains performance-related statistics for each team-season:
Takim (Team name) Sezon (Season) Galibiyet (Wins) Beraberlik (Draws) Maglubiyet (Losses) Atilan_Gol (Goals scored) Yenilen_Gol (Goals conceded) Averaj (Goal difference) Puan (Total points)

title: Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
text:
2.3 Reason of Choice
The choice of this dataset is motivated by several factors:

Relevance to Türkiye: The dataset focuses on the Turkish Super League, aligning with the course recommendation to use locally relevant data.
Richness and size: The dataset spans 20 seasons and includes multiple variables, making it suitable for in-depth analysis.
Real-world applicability: The relationship between financial investment and performance is a widely discussed topic in sports economics.
Availability of multiple dimensions: The dataset allows for combining financial, demographic, and performance variables.
Longitudinal analysis opportunity: The availability of long-term data enables trend analysis and structural comparisons across years.

This combination makes the dataset highly suitable for both exploratory and advanced analytical techniques.

title: Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
text:
2.4 Preprocessing
The datasets are stored as .csv files and imported into R. You can download the current version of the dataset in R format here.

title: Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
Financial Power vs Sporting Success: A 20-Year Analysis of the Turkish Super League
text:
3.4 Results
xxxxxx superlig_kadro_verileri <- read.csv(“data/superlig_kadro_verileri.csv”) superlig_puan_durumu_verileri <- read.csv(“data/superlig_puan_durumu_verileri.csv”) save(superlig_kadro_verileri, superlig_puan_durumu_verileri, file = “data/my_data.RData”) # 4. Results and Key Takeaways
xxxxxx

title: Assignment 1
text:
In this video (Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Mustafa Baydoğan & Erdi Daşdemir), main topic is about how data science intersects with industrial engineering. While watching it, I noticed something: data science is not a single profession as we often think. It actually includes different roles, and everyone does different things, but ultimately they all try to use data to make decisions. This is where industrial engineering comes into play. Someone studying this field is already familiar with concepts such as decision-making, optimization, and systems thinking. So transitioning into data science is not really like starting from scratch; it is more like applying what you already know to a different field.
One of the most emphasized points in the video is the difference between school and real life. At school, we usually work with clean data, but in real life data is not that organized. In fact, most of the time the data is incomplete, incorrect, or messy. Because of this, a large part of the work is spent cleaning and fixing the data before doing any analysis.
Another important point is that knowing Python or R alone is not enough. Technical knowledge is important, but it is not sufficient by itself. What really makes a difference is understanding the problem correctly and asking the right questions. Because even a correct result may be useless if the question behind it is wrong.
In our last class we also did something similar: we acknowledged that these techniques exist, but we did not go into the technical details. Instead, we focused on questions like what can be understood from this graph, how the data can be interpreted, and what the possible reasons behind edge cases might be.
Overall, the video gives the impression that data science is a process of exploration. There is no single correct path, and everyone finds their own path over time. Continuous learning is necessary.

title: Assignment 1
text:
In this video (Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Mustafa Baydoğan & Erdi Daşdemir), main topic is about how data science intersects with industrial engineering. While watching it, I noticed something: data science is not a single profession as we often think. It actually includes different roles, and everyone does different things, but ultimately they all try to use data to make decisions. This is where industrial engineering comes into play. Someone studying this field is already familiar with concepts such as decision-making, optimization, and systems thinking. So transitioning into data science is not really like starting from scratch; it is more like applying what you already know to a different field.
One of the most emphasized points in the video is the difference between school and real life. At school, we usually work with clean data, but in real life data is not that organized. In fact, most of the time the data is incomplete, incorrect, or messy. Because of this, a large part of the work is spent cleaning and fixing the data before doing any analysis.
Another important point is that knowing Python or R alone is not enough. Technical knowledge is important, but it is not sufficient by itself. What really makes a difference is understanding the problem correctly and asking the right questions. Because even a correct result may be useless if the question behind it is wrong.
In our last class we also did something similar: we acknowledged that these techniques exist, but we did not go into the technical details. Instead, we focused on questions like what can be understood from this graph, how the data can be interpreted, and what the possible reasons behind edge cases might be.
Overall, the video gives the impression that data science is a process of exploration. There is no single correct path, and everyone finds their own path over time. Continuous learning is necessary.

title: Assignment 1
text:
(b)
What is the importance of domain knowledge in data science?
“Domain knowledge helps in correctly understanding the problem and interpreting the results properly. Analyses performed without domain knowledge may be technically correct but can still lead to incorrect conclusions.”
According to the video, which of the following is the most important skill in data science?

Being able to write complex code
Memorizing algorithms
Understanding the problem correctly and asking the right questions (Correct)
Using the newest tools

title: Assignment 1
section: (c) Statistical Summaries with Custom Functions and Iteration Methods
text:
(c) Statistical Summaries with Custom Functions and Iteration Methods
In this section, I use the built-in mtcars dataset in R to compute summary statistics for each numerical column. I first define a custom summary function called compute_stats, then apply it using a for loop, sapply, and apply.

Custom Summary Function

compute_stats <- function(x) {
  data.frame(
    mean = mean(x),
    median = median(x),
    variance = var(x),
    IQR = IQR(x),
    minimum = min(x),
    maximum = max(x)
  )
}



Testing the Function on One Column

compute_stats(mtcars$mpg)

      mean median variance   IQR minimum maximum
1 20.09062   19.2  36.3241 7.375    10.4    33.9




Applying the Function Using a Loop

for (col_name in names(mtcars)) {
  cat("\n----------------------\n")
  cat("Column:", col_name, "\n")
  cat("----------------------\n")
  print(compute_stats(mtcars[[col_name]]))
}

title: Assignment 1
section: (d) Data Import, Missing Value Analysis, and Replacement
text:
(d) Data Import, Missing Value Analysis, and Replacement

Load the Dataset

library(dslabs)
data("polls_us_election_2016")



Define Personal Variables

my_first <- "Emrecan"
my_birth_year <- 2001



Compute the Value of k

sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))

[1] 11604




Number of NA Values per Column (Top 8)

na_per_column <- sort(colSums(is.na(polls_us_election_2016)), decreasing = TRUE)
head(na_per_column, 8)

rawpoll_mcmullin adjpoll_mcmullin  rawpoll_johnson  adjpoll_johnson 
            4178             4178             1409             1409 
           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 




Replace NA Values and Store the Result in a New Object

new_data <- polls_us_election_2016

for (col_name in names(new_data)) {
  if (is.numeric(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- my_birth_year + k
  } else if (is.character(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  } else if (is.factor(new_data[[col_name]])) {
    new_data[[col_name]] <- as.character(new_data[[col_name]])
    new_data[[col_name]][is.na(new_data[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
}



Display k Rows from the New Data Frame

  head(new_data, k)
} else {
  tail(new_data, k)
